skill.name: qwen-image
skill.description: Generate and edit images with Alibaba Qwen-Image-2.0 models through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Use Qwen-Image models for image generation, image editing, and text-focused visual designs.
skill.author: tul-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified extensive external command examples and network references, but these are aligned with the skill purpose of using inference.sh for Qwen image generation. The high-severity weak cryptography and system reconnaissance labels were dismissed as false positives based on the cited line context. No prompt injection attempt or malicious exfiltration intent was found in the reviewed file.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Execution for Image Generation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run infsh commands for login, app discovery, image generation, samples, and related skill installation. This matches the stated purpose and the allowed tool scope, but it still depends on a local external command and remote service behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents executable infsh and npx commands. The commands are not hidden or clearly malicious, but external execution is confirmed and should be disclosed.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Processing of Prompts and Images
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts and optional reference image URLs to inference.sh-hosted Qwen image applications. This is necessary for the service, but users should understand that prompts, image references, and generated outputs may be processed outside the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly references inference.sh and remote image URLs. The network behavior is expected for cloud inference, so this is a disclosure risk rather than evidence of abuse.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography hits appear to be false positives from model names, image field names, and table text. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, signature checks, or weak cryptographic algorithms in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines describe Qwen model names and parameter tables, not cryptographic operations. The semantic context strongly supports a false positive assessment.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The static reconnaissance hit points to the negative_prompt image parameter. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment inspection, file listing, or system profiling behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is a documented image-generation field for unwanted visual content. It has no system or environment reconnaissance semantics.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Execution for Image Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the agent to run infsh commands for login, app discovery, image generation, samples, and related skill installation. This matches the stated purpose and the allowed tool scope, but it still depends on a local external command and remote service behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents executable infsh and npx commands. The commands are not hidden or clearly malicious, but external execution is confirmed and should be disclosed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Network Processing of Prompts and Images
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends prompts and optional reference image URLs to inference.sh-hosted Qwen image applications. This is necessary for the service, but users should understand that prompts, image references, and generated outputs may be processed outside the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation explicitly references inference.sh and remote image URLs. The network behavior is expected for cloud inference, so this is a disclosure risk rather than evidence of abuse.

content.value_statement: Creating polished images often requires model-specific options and careful prompt structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Qwen-Image generation and editing with inference.sh.
actual_capabilities: Runs Qwen-Image-2.0 and Qwen-Image-2.0-Pro through the infsh CLI.
Creates images from text prompts with optional image counts and seeds.
Edits images with one to three reference image URLs.
Supports custom width and height within documented pixel limits.
Guides text-heavy poster prompts using the Pro model and prompt extension control.
Shows how to list Qwen image apps and generate sample input files.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated account.
Sends prompts and reference image URLs to a third-party inference service.
Does not include local model weights or offline generation.
Image quality and text accuracy depend on the selected Qwen model.

use_cases.title: Create concept visuals
use_cases.description: Generate several visual directions for product ideas, scenes, or campaign concepts.

use_cases.title: Draft text-focused posters
use_cases.description: Use the Pro model to create posters that include specified titles, subtitles, and layout details.

use_cases.title: Edit images with references
use_cases.description: Combine subject, clothing, style, or pose references into a guided image edit.

prompt_templates.title: Basic image prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Qwen-Image-2.0 to create one image of a calm mountain lake at sunrise. Use a natural color palette.

prompt_templates.title: Multiple creative options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Qwen-Image-2.0 to create four logo concept images for a modern coffee shop. Keep the style minimal and friendly.

prompt_templates.title: Text-heavy poster
prompt_templates.scenario: Use the Pro model for visible text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Qwen-Image-2.0-Pro to create a vertical poster with the title "Summer Sale" and subtitle "50% Off Everything". Keep prompt extension off.

prompt_templates.title: Reference image edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Qwen-Image-2.0 to edit three reference images. Preserve the person from the first image, outfit from the second image, and pose from the third image.

output_examples.input: Create a serene mountain landscape at sunset.
output_examples.output: Returns a PNG image result with a warm sunset scene, mountains, and calm landscape composition.

output_examples.input: Create four minimalist coffee shop logo concepts.
output_examples.output: Returns multiple PNG options suitable for comparing shapes, typography direction, and brand mood.

output_examples.input: Create a sale poster with exact headline and subtitle text.
output_examples.output: Returns a vertical poster image that prioritizes readable text placement and the requested promotional message.

best_practices: Use the Pro model when exact text rendering is important.
Set dimensions deliberately before generating posters, banners, or widescreen images.
Avoid sensitive prompts or private reference image URLs unless the external service is approved.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill when offline or local-only image generation is required.
Do not include secrets, private credentials, or confidential data in prompts.
Do not expect exact visual reproduction from vague prompts or low-quality references.

faq.question: What does this skill use to generate images?
faq.answer: It uses the inference.sh CLI to run Alibaba Qwen-Image-2.0 applications.

faq.question: Does it support image editing?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents editing with one to three reference image URLs.

faq.question: Which model should I use for text in images?
faq.answer: Use Qwen-Image-2.0-Pro for posters and other designs that need stronger text rendering.

faq.question: Can it generate multiple images at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented num_images option supports one to six generated images.

faq.question: Does this work without network access?
faq.answer: No. The skill depends on inference.sh and remote Qwen image applications.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning for external CLI execution and third-party network processing.
